<!--!!! THIS FILE IS AUTO-GENERATED. DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY. EDIT THE FILES IN THE SRCQMD DIR !!!-->

# The conditional sentence


::: {.callout-warning}
**THIS BOOK IS A WORK IN PROGRESS. IT IS INCOMPLETE AND MAY HAVE TYPOGRAPHICAL AND OTHER ERRORS.
IT IS NOT YET READY TO BE STUDIED FROM.**
:::


## Introduction

A conditional sentence is a special sentence, which consists of two parts:

1. A _condition_. The condition itself consists of two parts:

    i. The *condition-introducer*
   ii. The *condition-internal-sentence*

2. A _condition-response_. The condition-response can also consist of two parts:

    i. The *condition-response-introducer*. This may be absent for some conditional sentences.
   ii. The *condition-response-internal-sentence*

Here is an example of a conditional sentence in English:

(@) [If you come to me, then I will honor you.]{.trans}

@fig-cst_eng_ex1 shows the two parts of conditional sentence.

::: {#fig-cst_eng_ex1 .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=If you come to me then I will honor you.,
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, rotate=90, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=1.5em, xshift=-2em] {\scriptsize condition-introducer};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-2.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-internal-sentence};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=5em}, decorate] 
   (word-1.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-6em] {\scriptsize condition};

\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-6.south west) -- (word-6.south east)
      node [midway, below, rotate=90, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=1.1em, xshift=-2em] {\scriptsize c.response-introducer};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-7.south west) -- (word-10.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize c.response-internal-sentence};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=5em}, decorate] 
   (word-6.south west) -- (word-10.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=2cm, yshift=-6em] {\scriptsize condition-response};

\draw [dashed] (word-6.south west) -- ([yshift=-5em]word-6.south west);

\end{tikzpicture}
```
The parts of a conditional sentence.
:::

<!--
A conditional sentence is introduced by a condition-introducer, like "[if]{.trans}" in the example above.
Another common condition-introducer is "[when]{.trans}".
For example:
-->
<!--
Here is another example of a conditional sentence:

(@) [When the sun rises, the day begins.]{.trans}

Typically,
the statement in condition-response
is caused by the realization of the statement in the condition.
So, in example () above,
my honoring you is caused by,
and dependent on,
your honoring me.
And in example (), the day's beginning is caused by the rising of the sun.

However, the statement in the condition-response need not always be caused by the condition.^[[[النحو الوافي]{.ar} 4/422 footnote 1](https://shamela.ws/book/10641/2354)]
Sometimes the condition-response 
is only a reply, assessment, or confirmation of the assumption made in the condition.^[Fischer 229]
For example:

(@) [If this is so, well, such and such is the case.]{.trans}
    [Fischer 229]{.citex}  

(@) [If you honor me, then I already honored you yesterday.]{.trans}

In example (), the condition-response
[then I already honored you yesterday]{.trans}
is not caused by the realization of the condition
[if you honor me]{.trans}.
Rather it is only a response that completes the sentence.
-->

Conditional sentences in Arabic are introduced by _condition-introducers_ like:
[إِنْ]{.ar} [if]{.trans},
[لَوْ]{.ar} [if]{.trans},
[إِذَا]{.ar} [when]{.trans},
[مَنْ]{.ar} [who(ever)]{.trans},
[مَا]{.ar} [what(ever)]{.trans},
etc.

Amongst these condition-introducers,
[إِنْ]{.ar} is considered the most basic condition-introducer.^[[[شرح ابن يعيش على المفصل]{.ar} 5/106](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2044)]
We will study 
it
first.

## The [إِنْ]{.ar} conditional sentence

[إِنْ]{.ar} is considered the most basic of all the condition-introducers.^[sibawayhi]
It forms the basic conditional sentence.
Conditional sentences that employ other condition-introducers are based on
the conditional sentence with [إِنْ]{.ar}.

<!--
is used to form the _archetypal_ conditional sentence.
Other types of conditional sentence are formed based on the
model of
archetypal conditional sentence with [إِنْ]{.ar}.
-->

### The ø-state stateful verb with [إِنْ]{.ar}

In its basic form,
the conditional sentence consists of two parts:

1. a condition, consisting of:
   a. the condition-introducer [إِنْ]{.ar}, and 
   b. the condition-internal-sentence which is a verbal sentence consisting of (at least):
      * a ø-state stateful verb (the *condition-verb*) and
      * its doer
2. a condition-response, consisting of:
   a. the condition-response-internal-sentence which is a verbal sentence consisting of (at least):
      * a ø-state stateful verb (the *condition-response-verb*) and
      * its doer

   There is no condition-response-introducer.

<!--
In this archetypal form of the conditional sentence,
-->
The conditional particle [إِنْ]{.ar} directly
governs the condition-verb and the condition-response-verb in the ø-state.^[Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 3-4 citing [كتاب سيبويه]{.ar}]
Here is an example of an archetypal conditional sentence

(@) [إِنْ يَأْتِ زَيْدٌ يُكْرِمْهُ عَمْرٌو]{.ar}  
    [If Zayd would come, [#eamr]{.trn2} would honor him.]{.trans}  

<!--
In example () above,

  i. The conditional particle is [إِنْ]{.ar},
 ii. the condition is [تَأْتِنِي]{.ar}, and
iii. the condition-response is [آتِكَ]{.ar}.
-->

The conditional particle [إِنْ]{.ar} directly governs both
the condition-verb
[يَأْتِ]{.ar}
and the condition-response-verb
[يُكْرِمْ]{.ar}
(directly)
in the ø-state.
@fig-shartintatiniaatika depicts this government for example () above.

::: {#fig-shartintatiniaatika .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{عَمْرٌو} \txarb{يُكْرِمْهُ} \txarb{زَيْدٌ} \txarb{يَأْتِ} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-5.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=10pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize conditional particle};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-4.south west) -- (word-4.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-verb};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=6pt]word-2.south west) -- (word-2.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=-5pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-response-verb};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=40pt}, decorate] 
   (word-3.south west) -- ([xshift=5pt]word-4.south east) coordinate (bbb)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-45pt] {\scriptsize condition-internal-sentence};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=40pt}, decorate]
   (word-1.south west) -- (word-2.south east) coordinate (aaa)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-45pt] {\scriptsize condition-response-internal-sentence};

\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=90pt}, decorate] 
   (word-3.south west) -- (word-5.south east) coordinate (bbb)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-95pt] {\scriptsize condition};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror, raise=90pt}, decorate]
   (word-1.south west) -- (word-2.south east) coordinate (aaa)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-95pt] {\scriptsize condition-response};

\draw [dashed] ([xshift=-1pt]word-3.south west) -- ([xshift=-1pt,yshift=-90pt]word-3.south west);
\draw [dashed] (word-1.south west) -- ([yshift=-90pt]word-1.south west);
\draw [dashed] (word-5.south east) -- ([yshift=-90pt]word-5.south east);
\draw [dashed] ([xshift=5pt]word-4.south east) -- ([yshift=-40pt,xshift=5pt]word-4.south east);


\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=4pt,yshift=4pt] word-4.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=3pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=-4pt] word-2.north east);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=-4pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize u} (word-3.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=5pt] word-2.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize u} ([xshift=0pt] word-1.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=3pt]word-2.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize a} ([xshift=-9pt] word-2.north);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

A government diagram of a basic conditional sentence.
The arrows flow from governor to governee and are labelled with the state of the governee.
:::

Here are some more examples of the archetypal conditional sentence:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي آتِكَ]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/63](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/916)]{.citex}  
    [If you would come to me, I would come to you.]{.trans}

(@) [وإن تضرب أضرب]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/63](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/916)]{.citex}  
    [If you would beat, I would beat.]{.trans}

### The stateless verb with [إِنْ]{.ar}

A stateless verb can be used instead of one or both of the ø-state verbs in a conditional sentence.
So, for example, instead of saying:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي أُكْرِمْكَ]{.ar}

we can say, any one of:

(@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}

(@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أُكْرِمْكَ]{.ar}

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}

In general, all four options will have more-or-less the same meaning:
[If you come to me, I will come to you.]{.trans}.
In section\ (ref) below,
we will discuss the time-reference indicated by conditional sentences in more detail.
But for now we can generally consider
the stateless verb in a conditional sentence to lose its past time-reference.

For convenience,
in this,
and the following sections,
we will 
use
[قَتَلَ]{.ar}
to
refer to a stateless verb form
in either the
condition-verb
or the
condition-response-verb.
Similarly,
we will use [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar}, [يَقْتُلَ]{.ar} and [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} to refer to
u-state, a-state, and ø-state stateful verbs respectively.

Of the four options in examples () through (),
the combinations
[إِنْ يَقْتُلْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
and
[إِنْ قَتَلَ قَتَلَ]{.ar}
are most preferred.
This is because they exhibit
a symmetry between the
condition-verb
and the
condition-response-verb.^[peled]

The combination
[إِنْ قَتَلَ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar},
however,
is by no means uncommon.

But combination
<!--
in
example ()
[إِنْ تَأْتِنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar},
where the
condition-verb
is a
ø-state stateful verb,
and the
condition-response-verb
is a
stateless verb
-->
<!--
[إِنْ]{.ar} [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} [قَتَلَ]{.ar}
-->
[إِنْ يَقْتُلْ قَتَلَ]{.ar}
is the least commonly
found
in actual usage.
This is because when the
condition-verb
is
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
<!--
ø-state stateful verb,
-->
it prefers the presence of
another
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
<!--
ø-state stateful verb
-->
after it as the condition-response-verb.^[sibawayhi around 3/66]
Nevertheless,
this combination
is occasionally found,
as we show in example () below.


With regards to the government of the conditional sentences in examples () through (),
The condition-introducer [إِنْ]{.ar} will continue to govern
the condition-verb and the condition-response-verb in the ø-state directly,
regardless of whether the verbs are [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} or [قَتَلَ]{.ar}.
For a [قَتَلَ]{.ar} verb, [إِنْ]{.ar} will govern it in the positional ø-state.^[wafi]

In other words,
for example () above,
[إِنْ]{.ar} does not govern the verbal sentences
[أَتَيْتَنِي]{.ar}
and
[أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar} in the positional ø-state.
Rather,
the stateless verbs
[أَتَى]{.ar} and [أَكْرَمَ]{.ar}
are,
both times,
directly governed in the positional ø-state.^[haazimi]

@fig-in_ataytani_ataytuka depicts the government of example ().

::: {#fig-in_ataytani_ataytuka .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{أَكْرَمْتُكَ} \txarb{أَتَيْتَنِي} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-5.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=10pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize conditional particle};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=15pt]word-2.south west) -- (word-2.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=1em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-verb};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.5em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=-1.2em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-response-verb};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=5pt,yshift=1pt] word-2.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=5pt,yshift=1pt] word-1.north);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::




<!--
In section\ (ref), we will see how a past time-reference can be explicitly specified in the condition or condition-response if needed.

Of the four combinations in examples () through () above,
examples () and () are the strongest in their composition because they display a symmetry
between the condition-verb and the condition-response-verb.
The combination in example ()
of a stateless condition-verb and a stateful condition-response-verb is also fairly common.
The combination in example ()
of a stateful condition-verb and a stateless condition-response-verb
is weakest in its composition and is least commonly used.

It is, nevertheless, used occasionally.
For example:

(@) [مَنْ يَقُمْ لَيْلَةَ الْقَدْرِ إِيمَانًا وَاحْتِسَابًا غُفِرَ لَهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِنْ ذَنْبِهِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :35](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:35)]{.citex}  

TODO: Meaning of mudarie vs mudari3 from maani_nahw
-->

Here are some examples
from attested usage
of combinations of
stateless verbs
and stateful verbs
in conditional sentences:

[Get from Peled Cst pp. 16--17]

إِنْ أَذِنْتَ لِي أَعْطَيْتُ هَؤُلاَءِ
bukhari:2602

##### Examples of [إِنْ يَقْتُلْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}: {.unnumbered}

(@) [وَإِن تَعُودُوا۟ نَعُدۡ]{.quran} 8/19

##### Examples of [إِنْ قَتَلَ قَتَلَ]{.ar}: {.unnumbered}

(@) [إِنۡ أَحۡسَنتُمۡ أَحۡسَنتُمۡ لِأَنفُسِكُمۡۖ]{.quran} 17/7

(@) [وَإِنۡ عُدتُّمۡ عُدۡنَاۚ]{.quran} 17/8

##### Examples of [إِنْ قَتَلَ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}: {.unnumbered}

##### Examples of [إِنْ يَقْتُلْ قَتَلَ]{.ar}: {.unnumbered}


### Negating the condition or condition-response

When either of
the condition-verb or the condition-response-verb
are to be negated,
then we have two options:

 i. [لَا يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
ii. [لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}

<!--
The stateless verb
(with the negater [مَا]{.ar})
and the a-state stateful verb (with [لَنْ]{.ar})
-->
Negations
[مَا قَتَلَ]{.ar}
and
[لَنْ يَقْتُلَ]{.ar}
are not used when negating
the condition-verb or the condition-response-verb.^[[النحو الوافي]{.ar}]
(They can however be used when negating a verb
which is not directly
the condition-verb or the condition-response-verb, as we will see in section\ (ref), if Allāh wills.)

[لَا يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
is considered the negative counterpart of
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}.
And
[لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
is considered the negative counterpart of
[قَتَلَ]{.ar}.
And as with positive conditional sentences,
a symmetry between
the condition-verb and the condition-response
is preferred.

So if the condition is
[إِنْ قَتَلَ]{.ar},
then
[لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
is preferred in the condition-response.^[sirafi 3/295]
For example:

(@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي لَمْ آتِكَ]{.ar}

And
if the condition is
[إِنْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar},
then
[لَا يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
is preferred in the condition-response.^[sirafi 3/295]
For example:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي لَا آتِكَ]{.ar}


The non-symmetric combination
[إِنْ لَمْ يَقْتُلْ  يَقْتُلْ]{.ar},
(corresponding to
[إِنْ قَتَلَ  يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}),
is also fairly common.
For example:

(@) [إن لم تأتني أجزك]{.ar}
    [sibawahi 3/68]{.citex}  

But as with the combination
[إِنْ يَقْتُلْ  قَتَلَ]{.ar}
in section\ (ref) above,
the combination
[إِنْ يَقْتُلْ  لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
is uncommon.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي لَمْ آتِكَ]{.ar}
    [sirafi 3/295]{.citex}  

In any case,
symmetry is by no means mandatory,
and the longer the distance (in words) between
the condition-verb and the condition-response,
the easier it is to disregard its being preferred.^[rummani]

With regards to
their frequencies in attested usage,
option (i)
[لَا يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
is possibly the more original,^[Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 17]
but it is rarer in usage.
Here are some examples:

(@) [إِلَّا تَفۡعَلُوهُ تَكُن فِتۡنَةࣱ فِی ٱلۡأَرۡضِ وَفَسَادࣱ كَبِیرࣱ]{.quran}
    [[[سورة الأنفال]{.ar} 8:73](https://quran.com/8/73) cited by Reckendorf, _Arabische syntax_ 487]{.citex}  
    [If you do not do so [i.e., ally yourselves with other believers], there will be fitnah [i.e., disbelief and oppression] on earth and great corruption.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنْ تَدْفِنُوا الدَّاءَ **لَا نُخْفِهِ**]{.ar}
    [Reckendorf, _Arabische syntax_ 487]{.citex}  

(@) [**إِنْ لَا تَكُنْ** هِنْدٌ بَكَتْهُ فَقَدْ بَكَتْ عَلَيْهِ الثُّرَيَّا]{.ar}
    [Reckendorf, _Arabische syntax_ 487]{.citex}  
    [If Hind did not weep [for] him, then the Pleiades have wept over him]{.trans}  
    (The condition-response in this example begins with [فَ]{.ar}. We will discuss this in section\ (ref) below.)  

(@) [اللهُمّ إنْ تَظْهَرْ عَلَيّ هَذِهِ الْعِصَابَةُ يَظْهَرْ الشّرْكُ، **وَلَا يَقُمْ** لَك دِين]{.ar}
    [[[مغازي الواقدي]{.ar} 1/67](https://shamela.ws/book/23680/108) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 15]{.citex}  

The particle [لَا]{.ar}
will not affect the government of
the condition-verb or the condition-response-verb.
The conditional particle [إِنْ]{.ar} will continue to govern these verbs directly in the ø-state.
@fig-in_laa depicts the government of the negated condition-response-verb in example () above.

::: {#fig-in_laa .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{نُخْفِهِ} \txarb{لَا} \txarb{الدَّاءَ} \txarb{تَدْفِنُوا} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-5.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=10pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize conditional particle};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1em]word-4.south west) -- (word-4.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-verb};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=0.75em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-response-verb};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=0pt,yshift=1pt] word-4.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=0pt,yshift=1pt] word-1.north);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::

Compared to option\ (i)
[لَا يَقْتُلْ]{.ar},
option\ (ii)
[لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
is by far the more common technique to negate
the condition-verb and the condition-response-verb.

Just like the case for [قَتَلَ]{.ar},
when
[لَمْ]{.ar} is used to negate
the condition-verb or the condition-response-verb,
it also generally loses its past time-reference.

Here are some examples of
the condition-verb and the condition-response-verb,
negated with [لَمْ]{.ar}:


(@) [إن **أتيتني** لم آتك]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/91](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/944)]{.citex}  

(@) [إن **لم تأتني** أجزك]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/68](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/921)]{.citex}  

(@) [إن **لم تأتني لم آتك**]{.ar}

(@) [إِنِ افْتَرَقْتُمْ **لَمْ تَجْتَمِعُوا** أَبَدًا]{.ar}
    [[[تاريخ الطبري]{.ar} 4/24](https://shamela.ws/book/9783/1915) cited by Reckendorf, _Arabische syntax_ 485]{.citex}  

The grammarians differed regarding
the government of the 
the condition-verb and the condition-response-verb,
when negated with [لَمْ]{.ar}.^[[النحو الوافي]{.ar}]
Some grammarians are of the opinion that
[لَمْ]{.ar} directly governs the ø-state mudarie verb
while [إِنْ]{.ar} governs the verbal sentences which contain the stateful verb.
This government is depicted in
@fig-gov_in_lam_a for example ().

::: {#fig-gov_in_lam_a .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{آتِكَ} \txarb{لَمْ} \txarb{تَأْتِنِي} \txarb{لَمْ} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

\draw [decoration={brace,raise=2em}, decorate] 
   (word-1.north west) -- (word-2.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};
\draw [decoration={brace,raise=2em}, decorate] 
   (word-3.north west) -- (word-4.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};

\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.1em]word-3.south west) -- (word-3.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-verb};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=0.85em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-response-verb};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=.5em,yshift=0.3em] word-3.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-2.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=.5em,yshift=0.3em] word-1.north);

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=-1.5em,yshift=2.5em] word-4.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=1em,yshift=2.5em] word-1.north);

\draw [dashed] ([xshift=-1pt]word-3.north west) -- ([xshift=-1pt,yshift=2em]word-3.north west);
\draw [dashed] (word-1.north west) -- ([yshift=2em]word-1.north west);
\draw [dashed] (word-4.north east) -- ([yshift=2em]word-4.north east);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::

Other grammarians were of the opinion that [إِنْ]{.ar} would continue to govern
the condition-verb and the condition-response-verb directly,
and that [لَمْ]{.ar} has no governing effect in this case.
This is depicted in
@fig-gov_in_lam_b for the same example ().

::: {#fig-gov_in_lam_b .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{آتِكَ} \txarb{لَمْ} \txarb{تَأْتِنِي} \txarb{لَمْ} \txarb{إِنْ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);


\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.1em]word-3.south west) -- (word-3.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-verb};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=0.85em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-response-verb};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=.25em,yshift=.5em] word-3.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-5.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=0.25em,yshift=.5em] word-1.north);

\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::

### The condition-response that begins with [فَ]{.ar}

[Maybe make it a top level section after idha?]

So far, we have studied

### The inverted conditional sentence

[[النحو الوافي]{.ar} 4/450-451 (footnote); Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 139--140; [شرح ابن يعيش على المفصل]{.ar} 5/118]

Oftentimes,
we come across an _inverted_ conditional sentence,
where what seems to be the condition-response comes before the condition.
For example:

(@) [آتِيكَ إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى]{.ar}
    [[[شرح ابن يعيش على المفصل]{.ar} 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [I will come to you if you come to me.]{.trans}  

In example () above, it would seem that
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
is the condition-response
and
[إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى]{.ar}
is the condition.

But actually,
the mainstream view amongst the grammarians is that
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
cannot be a condition-response because the
condition-response is not allowed to precede the condition.

This view is supported by the fact that the
verb in
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
is in the u-state.
If it were a condition-response-verb then it would be in the ø-state, thus:
[آتِكَ]{.ar}.
But
a ø-state stateful verb in the beginning of sentence,
followed by a condition is not a correctly formed sentence.
So we cannot say, for example:

(@) ✗ [آتِكَ إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى]{.ar}
    [[[شرح ابن يعيش على المفصل]{.ar} 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  

The way the grammarians solve
the analysis of example () is
by considering
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
an independent sentence
that is
structurally complete.
[إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى]{.ar}
is then considered to be a condition.
Its
condition-response
[[آتِكَ]]{.ar}
is virtually modeled
after it.
The virtually modeled condition-response
is deleted in surface speech
and its meaning is indicated by the preceding independent sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}.

The complete sentence with the virtual model is:

(@) [آتِيكَ إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى [آتِكَ]]{.ar}

<!--
The virtual model of the deleted condition-response is 
[آتِكَ]{.ar}.
This rendition of the virtual model is suggested by the preceding independent sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}.
-->

But how is the conditional sentence
[إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى [آتِكَ]]{.ar}
connected
to the preceding independent sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}?
One opinion
is that the conditional sentence
[إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِى [آتِكَ]]{.ar}
can be considered to be
attached to (i.e. governed by) the verb in
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
in much the same way as an adverb of time/place or preposition would be.
For example, in:

(@) [آتيك يومَ الجمعة]{.ar}
    [[[شرح ابن يعيش على المفصل]{.ar} 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [I will come to you the day of Friday.]{.trans}  

<!--
@fig-inverted_shart depicts this theorized government of example ().

TODO: think about if [إِنْ]{.ar} itself should be governed by [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar} and not the entire conditional sentence.
Then it will be more in line with prepositions.
But then how about sentences like
[اضرب مَنْ يقُمْ]{.ar},
if indeed they are valid?
On the other hand [آتيك في البيت]{.ar} is similar to [آتيك عند البيت]{.ar}, so then [في]{.ar}
(and [إِنْ]{.ar}) should be governed directly by the verb.


::: {#fig-inverted_shart .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{[آتِكَ]} \txarb{أَتَيْتَنِي} \txarb{إِنْ} \txarb{آتِيكَ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);


\draw [decoration={brace,raise=2.3em}, decorate] 
   (word-1.north west) -- (word-3.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.5em]word-2.south west) -- (word-2.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=0.2em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-verb};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   ([xshift=1.1em]word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=-0.2em, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-response-verb};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=1pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=.25em,yshift=.5em] word-2.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize ø} ([xshift=0.25em,yshift=.5em] word-1.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=2pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=2] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {} ([xshift=2.4em,yshift=2.7em] word-1.north);

\draw [dashed] (word-1.north west) -- ([yshift=2.3em]word-1.north west);
\draw [dashed] (word-3.north east) -- ([yshift=2.3em]word-3.north east);
\end{tikzpicture}

```

:::
-->

Having said all the above,
there is no problem if we refer to such a sentence,
loosely speaking and for convenience,
as an inverted conditional sentence.^[[النحو الوافي]{.ar} citing [كتاب سيبويه]{.ar}]
As long as we understand that the true condition-response is deleted and virtually modeled after the condition in sentence word order.

Here are some more examples of inverted conditional sentences:


(@) [أًحْسِنُ إليك إن أكرمتَنى]{.ar}
    [[[شرح ابن يعيش على المفصل]{.ar} 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [I will be good to you if you honor me.]{.trans}  

(@) [أنتِ طالقٌ إن دخلتِ الدارَ]{.ar}
    [[[شرح ابن يعيش على المفصل]{.ar} 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [You~f~ are divorced if you enter the house.]{.trans}  

(@) [أنا ظالمٌ إن فعلتُ]{.ar}
    [[[شرح ابن يعيش على المفصل]{.ar} 5/118](https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2056)]{.citex}  
    [I [will be] a wrongdoer if I did [some action].]{.trans}  


In examples () and () above,
the "inverted" condition-responses
are nounal sentences
([أَنْتِ طَالِقٌ]{.ar}
and
[أَنَا ظَالِمٌ]{.ar})
, not verbal sentences.
We will discuss such condition-responses in section\ (ref), if Allāh wills.

[find [إن شئت]{.ar} at the end of a sentence in hadith]

<!--
(@) [وإن أتاه خليلٌ يومَ مسألةٍ ... يقولُ لا غائبٌ مالي ولا حَرِمُ]{.ar}
    [[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/66]{.citex}  
-->


Note that all the examples
of inverted conditional sentences,
that we have given so far
have
[إِنْ قَتَلَ]{.ar}
in the condition
and not
[إِنْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}.
This is because,
as we mentioned in section\ (ref) above,
when the
condition-verb
is
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
it prefers the presence of
another
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
after it as the condition-response-verb.
So the following example is not preferred:

(@) [آتيك إن تأتني]{.ar}
    [[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/68]{.citex}  

This is because
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
in the condition
([إن تأتني]{.ar})
prefers 
that it have another [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
in the condition-response,
and that its condition-response
not be deleted.^[ibn ya3ish, sibawayhi 3/66]

Having said that, an inverted
[إِنْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
condition
could very rarely be found.^[Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 143]

[إِنْ لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
in an inverted conditional sentence
is,
however,
fine.
This is
because it is the negative counterpart to
[إِنْ قَتَلَ]{.ar}.
For example,

(@) [وَاعْلَمِي أَنِّي امْرُؤٌ سَأَمُوتُ إنْ لَمْ أُقْتَلِ]{.ar}
    [[عنترة]{.ar} cited by Reckendorf, _Arabische syntax_ 488]{.citex}  
    [Know that I am a person-- I will die if I am not killed.]{.trans}  

TODO: get more examples from [Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 141ff]

---

TODO: condition-verb is preferred to be stateless or with [لَمْ]{.ar}, not positive ø-state stateful

::: {.ar}
واعلم أنّه لا يحسن أن تقول: "آتيك إن تأتني" ؛ لأنك جزمت بـ "إن" . وإذا أعملتها، لم يكن بدٌّ من الجواب، ولم تأتِ بجواب، ولو قلت. "أتيتك إن أتيتني" ، جاز؛ لأن حرف الشرط لم يجزم فساغ أن لا تأتي بجواب
:::

[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar}](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1):

::: {.ar}
وقبح في الكلام أن تعمل إن أو شئ من حروف الجزاء في الأفعال حتى تجزمه في اللفظ ثم لا يكون لها جواب ينجزم بما قبله. ألا ترى أنك تقول: آتيك إن أتيتني، ولا تقول آتيك إن تأتني، إلا في شعر، لأنك أخرت إن وما عملت فيه ولم تجعل لإن جوابا ينجزم بما قبله.
:::

next couple of pages also good



### A u-state stateful condition-response-verb


Occassionally we see a conditional sentence
of the form
[إِنْ قَتَلَ يَقْتُلُ]{.ar}.
That is, a stateful condition-response-verb is in the u-state,
not the ø-state.
For example:

(@) [إن أتيتني آتيك]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/66](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1)]{.citex}  
    [If you come to me, I come to you.]{.trans}  

This structure again requires virtual modeling to analyze it.
This is
because the underlying principle that the grammarians have theorized
for the conditional sentence,
is that
the condition-introducer [إِنْ]{.ar}
governs
both the condition-verb
and the condition-response-verb
in the ø-state.

One way the grammarians work around this problem is by modeling example ()
to originally be an inverted conditional sentence, thus:

(@) [آتيك إن أتيتني [آتك]]{.ar}  
    [I come to you if you come to me [I come to you].]{.trans}  

Once the original condition-response
[آتِكَ]{.ar}
is deleted,
then the independent sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar}
is (optionally)
moved to after the condition to take the place of
the original condition-response
[آتِكَ]{.ar}.^[[النحو الوافي]{.ar} 4/475--476 (footnotes)]

There is also another technique to analyze such conditional sentences,
and that is to assume a deleted [فَ]{.ar}
(and also possibly a deleted subject)
before the condition-response.^[usool fi al-nahw 2/194; rummani 949; [معاني النحو]{.ar} 4/104]
With the
deleted elements restored,
restored, the sentence is:

(@) [إن أتيتني فآتيك]{.ar}  
    or  
    [إن أتيتني فأنا آتيك]{.ar}  
    [If you come to me **then** I will come to you.]{.trans}  

Condition-responses that begin with [فَ]{.ar} will be treated in section\ (ref) below, if Allāh wills.

Here are some more examples of this kind of conditional sentence:

(@) [**وإن أتاه** خليلٌ يومَ مسألةٍ ... **يقولُ** لا غائبٌ مالي ولا حَرِمُ]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/66](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/919#p1)]{.citex}  
    [And if a friend came to him on a day of asking, he says, "My wealth is neither absent nor denied."]{.trans}

(@) [قَالَتِ اللَّهُمَّ **إِنْ يَمُتْ يُقَالُ** هِيَ قَتَلَتْهُ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :2217](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2217) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 17]{.citex}  
    [She said, "O Allāh if he should die, it will be said, 'She killed him.'"]{.trans}  


Note again,
that the condition-verb in these examples has been [قَتَلَ]{.ar},
not [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}.
This is again consistent
with what we mentioned in sections\ (ref) and (ref),
that when the condition-verb is [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
then it prefers the presence of another [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} as the condition-response-verb.

Having said that,
a conditional sentence of the form
[إِنْ يَقْتُلْ يَقْتُلُ]{.ar}
is found on occasion, often by poetic license.^[[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/67](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/920#p1)]
For example:

(@) [يا أَقْرَعُ بنَ حابسٍ يا أَقْرَعُ ... إنَّك **إن يُصْرَعْ أخوك تُصْرَعُ**]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/67](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/920#p1)]{.citex}  
    [O [#aqrae]{.trn2} son of [#HAbis]{.trn2}, O [#aqrae]{.trn2}! Indeed if your brother would be thrown down, you are being thrown down.]{.trans}  

## The [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentence

### The original temporal signification of [إِذَا]{.ar}

[إِذَا]{.ar} "[when]{.trans}"
is a noun which originally has a
temporal (time) signification
rather than a conditional signification.^[Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 24 citing [[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/60](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/913#p1); [[المقتضب للمبرد]{.ar} 2/55-56](https://shamela.ws/book/6965/324#p1)]
For example,

(@) [آتِيكَ إِذَا احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/60](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/913#p1)]{.citex}  
    [I will come to you when the unripe dates redden.]{.trans}

In example () above,
the
u-state stateful verb
[آتِي]{.ar}
signifies its usual imperfect aspect
[I come/will come]{.trans}.
This is because example () is technically not a conditional sentence.
[إِذَا]{.ar} signifies
"[when]{.trans}", not as a condition which may or may not occur,
but as an event which is expected to occur.


[إِذَا]{.ar} is in the a-state as a adverb of place.
It is governed by the verb [آتِي]{.ar}.
[إِذَا]{.ar} is also a annexe noun.
Its (positional) base noun is
the following verbal sentence
[احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}.
<!--
A word-for-word translation that indicates this government is:
[I will come to you **at the time of when** the unripe dates redden]{.trans}
-->
The government of example () above is depicted in @fig-aatika_idha_hmarra_lbusr.

::: {#fig-aatika_idha_hmarra_lbusr .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{الْبُسْرُ} \txarb{احْمَرَّ} \txarb{إِذَا} \txarb{آتِيكَ},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-5.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=10pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize conditional particle};
%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-3.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition};
\draw [decoration={brace}, decorate] 
   (word-1.north west) -- (word-2.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=3pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize a} ([xshift=2pt] word-3.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=-2pt] word-3.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize i} ([xshift=-13pt,yshift=4pt] word-2.north);

%\path (0,0) node [midway, below=7.5ex, right=0ex, align=left] {``I read the small books.''};

\end{tikzpicture}

```
:::

A [قَتَلَ]{.ar} verbal sentence
typically does not hold on to its past time-reference
when it is the positional base noun of
[إِذَا]{.ar}.
That is why we have translated
[احْمَرَّ]{.ar} in example () as
[redden]{.trans},
not [reddened]{.trans}.

<!--
[إِذَا]{.ar} usually indicates a future time.
[إِذَا]{.ar} is also most commonly followed by a stateless verb
in its positional base noun,
(like [احْمَرَّ]{.ar} in example () above).
But because of the future signification of [إِذَا]{.ar} the stateless verb will (usually) give a future meaning:
[when the unripe dates [**will**] redden]{.trans}.

The past time-reference equivalent of example () above is:

(@) [أَتَيْتُكَ إِذِ احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}  
    [I **came** to you when the unripe dates **reddened**.]{.trans}

The adverb of place
[إِذْ]{.ar}
usually indicates a past time just like
[إِذَا]{.ar} usually indicates a future time.
-->

### Conditional signification of [إِذَا]{.ar}

Example () above can be transformed into a conditional sentence.
This is done by taking [إِذَا]{.ar} and its positional base noun
[احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar},
and putting them in the front of
the sentence,
<!--
the verbal sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar},
-->
thus:

(@) [إِذَا احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ أَتَيْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    [When the unripe dates redden, I will come to you.]{.trans}

Note that we have also replaced the [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar} verbal sentence
[آتِيكَ]{.ar} in example ()
with the [قَتَلَ]{.ar} verbal sentence
[أَتَيْتُ]{.ar}.
This is because
now that example () is a conditional sentence
(with the condition-response following
the condition),
[قَتَلَ]{.ar} as the condition-response-verb also does not hold on to its past time-reference meaning.

<!--
This is because when [إِذَا]{.ar} is a condition-introducer in a conditional sentence,
then, like [إِنْ]{.ar},
it requires verbal sentences in both the condition and the condition-response.
But whereas [إِنْ]{.ar} allows both (ø-state) stateful verbs and stateless verbs,
[إِذَا]{.ar} strongly prefers stateless verbs in both clauses.
Because of the typical future signification of [إِذَا]{.ar},
both stateless verbs will usually have a future meaning.
-->

Also unlike [إِنْ]{.ar}, 
even when [إِذَا]{.ar} is a condition-introducer,
it does not (directly) govern either of the verbs in the condition or the condition-response.
Rather, [إِذَا]{.ar} governs entire verbal sentence in the condition in an annexation.
And [إِذَا]{.ar} itself is governed by the verb in the condition-response.^[Some grammarians disagreed. Give Abu Hayyan's opinion.]
@fig-idha_hmarra_lbusru_ataytuka
depicts the government of example ().

::: {#fig-idha_hmarra_lbusru_ataytuka .test-class}

```{.tikz}
\usetikzlibrary{decorations.text, decorations.pathreplacing}
\begin{tikzpicture}%[nodes={text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex}]
\path [decoration={text effects along path, 
  text=\txarb{أَتَيْتُكَ} \txarb{الْبُسْرُ} \txarb{احْمَرَّ} \txarb{إِذَا},
  text effects/.cd, 
    path from text, text along path,
    text depth=0.25ex,text height=1.25ex,
    group letters, word count=\w,
    every word/.style={name=word-\w, execute at begin node=\strut}},
  decorate] (0,0);

%\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
%   (word-5.south west) -- (word-5.south east)
%      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=10pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize conditional particle};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-2.south west) -- (word-4.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=5pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition};
\draw [decoration={brace, mirror}, decorate] 
   (word-1.south west) -- (word-1.south east)
      node [midway, below, text centered,text width=1cm, xshift=-5pt, yshift=-5pt] {\scriptsize condition-response};
\draw [decoration={brace}, decorate] 
   (word-2.north west) -- (word-3.north east)
      node [midway, above] {};

\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=3pt] word-1.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize a} ([xshift=2pt] word-4.north);
\draw[->,decorate] ([xshift=-2pt] word-4.north) to [in=90,out=90,looseness=1] node[midway,inner sep=0.5pt,fill=white] {\scriptsize i} ([xshift=-13pt,yshift=4pt] word-3.north);

%\path (0,0) node [midway, below=7.5ex, right=0ex, align=left] {``I read the small books.''};

\end{tikzpicture}

```
:::

Here are some more examples of conditional sentences with [إِذَا]{.ar}:

### Stateful verbs in the [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentence

We learned
in section\ (ref) above,
that the condition-introducer [إِنْ]{.ar}
governs the condition-verb and the condition-response-verb in the ø-state.

The condition-introducer [إِذَا]{.ar}, however,
does not govern either the condition-verb or condition-response-verb,
as we saw in @fig-idha_hmarra_lbusru_ataytuka above.
So [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} is not usually used in [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences.
The most common form for
[إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences,
as we saw in example () above,
is
[إِذَا قَتَلَ قَتَلَ]{.ar}.

Having said that,
we do often find
the _u-state_ stateful verb
[يَقْتُلُ]{.ar}
as either or as both
the condition-verb
and the condition-response-verb.
Here are some examples:

<!--
(@) [إذا زالت الشمس **آتيك**]{.ar}
    [[[الإيضاح في علوم البلاغة للقزويني]{.ar} 2/117](https://shamela.ws/book/7380/324)]{.citex}  
    [When the sun declines, I will come to you.]{.trans}
-->

(@) [إِذَا **تُتۡلَىٰ** عَلَیۡهِمۡ ءَایَـٰتُ ٱلرَّحۡمَـٰنِ خَرُّوا۟ سُجَّدࣰا وَبُكِیࣰّا]{.quran}
    [[[سورة مريم]{.ar} 19:58](https://quran.com/19/58) cited by Reckendorf, _Die syntaktischen Verhältnisse des Arabischen_ 648]{.citex}  

(@) [إِذَا **تَكُونُ** كَرِيهَةٌ **أُدْعَى** لَهَا]{.ar}
    [cited by Reckendorf, _Die syntaktischen Verhältnisse des Arabischen_ 647]{.citex}  
    [When there is an inconvenience, I am called to it.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنّ الكريم إذا **يُحَرَّبُ يَغضَبُ**]{.ar}
    [cited by Reckendorf, _Die syntaktischen Verhältnisse des Arabischen_ 647]{.citex}  
    [The noble person -- when he is provoked, he becomes angry.]{.trans}  

(@) [وإذا **تَضْحَكُ تُبْدي** حَبباً]{.ar}
    [cited by Reckendorf, _Die syntaktischen Verhältnisse des Arabischen_ 647--648]{.citex}  
    [When she laughs, she shows musky bubbles.]{.trans}  

Very rarely, we also find [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} used in [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences.
For example:

(@) [إِذَا أَخَذْتُمَا مَضَاجِعَكُمَا **تُكَبِّرَا** أَرْبَعًا وَثَلاَثِينَ **وَتُسَبِّحَا** ثَلاَثًا وَثَلاَثِينَ **وَتَحْمَدَا** ثَلاَثَةً وَثَلاَثِينَ فَهْوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمَا مِنْ خَادِمٍ]{.ar}  
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :3705](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3705) cited by [[شواهد التوضيح لابن مالك]{.ar} 71](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/68#p1)]{.citex}  
    [When you go to bed, you magnify thirty-four times, and glorify thirty-three times, and praise thirty-three times.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِذَا **تُصِبْكَ** خَصَاصَةٌ  فَتَجَمَّلِ]{.ar}
    [Wright 2/12B]{.citex}  
    [When poverty befalls you then bear it patiently.]{.trans}  
    ([تَجَمَّلِ]{.ar} in rhyme for [تَجَمَّلْ]{.ar}.)  

### Negation in the [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentence

When the negating the condition-verb
or the condition-response-verb
in [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences,
the form
[لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
is used.

The form
[لَا يَقْتُلُ]{.ar}
can also be used to negate
the condition-response-verb.

Here are some examples:

(@) [إِنَّ مِمَّا أَدْرَكَ النَّاسُ مِنْ كَلاَمِ النُّبُوَّةِ الأُولَى إِذَا **لَمْ تَسْتَحِي** فَاصْنَعْ مَا شِئْتَ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :6120](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:6120)]{.citex}  
    [One of the sayings of the early prophets which the people have got is: If you don't feel ashamed, then do whatever you like.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِذَا **لَمْ يَجِدِ** الْمَاءَ **لاَ يُصَلِّي**]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :345](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:345) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 28]{.citex}  
    [If he doesn't find water, he doesn't pray?]{.trans}  

(@) [أَلَيْسَ إِذَا حَاضَتْ **لَمْ تُصَلِّ وَلَمْ تَصُمْ**]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :304](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:304)]{.citex}  
    [Is it not the case [that] when she mensurates, she does not pray, and she does not fast?]{.trans}  

### The inverted conditional sentence with [إِذَا]{.ar}

Let's go back to example () above:

(@) [آتِيكَ إِذَا احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}
    [[[كتاب سيبويه]{.ar} 3/60](https://shamela.ws/book/23018/913#p1)]{.citex}  
    [I will come to you when the unripe dates redden.]{.trans}

Technically, this is not a conditional sentence.
Nor does it technically contain a conditional sentence.
This is
because a conditional sentence,
according to our definition,
requires that the condition-response follow the condition in sentence word order.
But there is no condition-response,
whether surface or virtually modeled,
after
[إِذَا احْمَرَّ الْبُسْرُ]{.ar}.
(See the government of this sentence in @fig-aatika_idha_hmarra_lbusr.)

Having said that,
there is no problem if we refer to such a sentence,
loosely speaking and for convenience,
as an inverted conditional sentence.
if contains the _meaning_ of a condition.
For example:

(@) [فَأَقْبَلَ رَجُلٌ غَائِرُ الْعَيْنَيْنِ مُشْرِفُ الْوَجْنَتَيْنِ نَاتِئُ الْجَبِينِ كَثُّ اللِّحْيَةِ مَحْلُوقٌ فَقَالَ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَا مُحَمَّدُ فَقَالَ  **مَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ إِذَا عَصَيْتُ** أَيَأْمَنُنِي اللَّهُ عَلَى أَهْلِ الأَرْضِ فَلاَ تَأْمَنُونِي]{.ar}^[It is not clear to us why [يُطِع]{.ar} is in the ø-state from this source. Peled's source [الجامع الصحيح]{.ar}, ed.\ L.\ Krehl, Leiden: Brill, 1864, II, 337 l:9 has [يُطِيعُ]{.ar} in the u-state.]  
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :3344](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3344) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 142]{.citex}  
    [Then a man with sunken eyes, prominent checks, a raised forehead, a thick beard and a shaven head, came in front [of the Prophet [ﷺ]{.aralt}] and said, "Be afraid of Allāh, O [#muHammad]{.trn2}!" The Prophet [ﷺ]{.aralt} said, "Who would obey Allah **if** I disobeyed [Him]? (Is it fair that) Allāh has trusted all the people of the earth to me while you do not trust me?"]{.trans}  

### Certainty of the event indicated by [إِذَا]{.ar}

[إِذَا]{.ar} is typically used when the condition is certain to occur.
The English expression,
"It's not a matter of _if_, but _when_,"
also indicates the fundamental difference between _if_ and _when_ conditional sentences.
So, for example,
if you say:

(@) [إن تكرمني أكرمك]{.ar}
    [[[الإيضاح في علوم البلاغة للقزويني]{.ar} 2/117](https://shamela.ws/book/7380/324)]{.citex}  
    [If you would honor me, I would honor you.]{.trans}  

then you are not certain if the other person would honor you or not.
But if you say:

(@) [إذا زالت الشمس **آتيك**]{.ar}
    [[[الإيضاح في علوم البلاغة للقزويني]{.ar} 2/117](https://shamela.ws/book/7380/324)]{.citex}  
    [When the sun declines, I will come to you.]{.trans}

then you are certain that the condition
[إذا زالت الشمس]{.ar}
will, at some point, be valid.^[[[الإيضاح في علوم البلاغة للقزويني]{.ar} 2/117](https://shamela.ws/book/7380/324)]

Having said that,
[إِذَا]{.ar} may not always indicate the certainness of the event in the condition.
For example:

(@) [فَأَقْبَلَ رَجُلٌ غَائِرُ الْعَيْنَيْنِ مُشْرِفُ الْوَجْنَتَيْنِ نَاتِئُ الْجَبِينِ كَثُّ اللِّحْيَةِ مَحْلُوقٌ فَقَالَ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ يَا مُحَمَّدُ فَقَالَ  **مَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ إِذَا عَصَيْتُ** أَيَأْمَنُنِي اللَّهُ عَلَى أَهْلِ الأَرْضِ فَلاَ تَأْمَنُونِي]{.ar}  
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :3344](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3344)]{.citex}  
    [Then a man with sunken eyes, prominent checks, a raised forehead, a thick beard and a shaven head, came in front [of the Prophet [ﷺ]{.aralt}] and said, "Be afraid of Allāh, O [#muHammad]{.trn2}!" The Prophet [ﷺ]{.aralt} said, "Who would obey Allah **if** I disobeyed [Him]? (Is it fair that) Allāh has trusted all the people of the earth to me while you do not trust me?"]{.trans}  

Even though [إِذَا]{.ar} is used in the above [HadIv]{.trn2}, it does not indicate a certain event.
Far from it.
In fact, there is no question that the Prophet [ﷺ]{.aralt} would never disobey Allāh.

In such cases, [إِذَا]{.ar} can retain its temporal signification,
but the event in the condition would not be considered certain.

<!--
### [إِذَا]{.ar} indicating a past time

(@) [وَإِذَا رَأَوۡا۟ تِجَـٰرَةً أَوۡ لَهۡوًا ٱنفَضُّوۤا۟ إِلَیۡهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَاۤىِٕمࣰاۚ]{.quran}
    [[[سورة الجمعة]{.ar} 62:11](https://quran.com/62/11)]{.citex}  
    [But [on one occasion] when they saw a transaction or a diversion, [O Muḥammad], they rushed to it and left you standing.]{.trans}  
-->

## Time-reference

For conditional sentences which have a condition-verb or condition-response-verb,
the time-reference of either of these two verbs
is determined by context, regardless of whether the verb is in the
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} or [قَتَلَ]{.ar} form.

Most contexts will steer the time-reference to the future.
For example:

in ataytani akramtuka

in ta'tini ukrimka

But in some contexts,
the time-reference can be the past.
Examples:


The time-reference can also be the present.
This is usually for _static_ verbs...


When the condition-response is introduced by [فَ]{.ar},
then
it is treated as an independent sentence signifying a time-reference just like a non-conditional sentence would.

[Maybe make it a top level section after idha?]

[Has to be after MSC because [in kaana qad qatala]{.trans} has to be followed by fa]

فإن قيل: فإنّهم يقولون:"إن كنتَ زُرْتَنِي أمسِ أكرمتُك اليومَ"، وقد وقع بعد "إن" الفعلُ، ومعناه المضيُّ، ومنه قوله تعالى: {إِنْ كُنْتُ قُلْتُهُ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُ} (١)، قيل: قد أجاب عن ذلك المبرد، وقال: إنّما ساغ ذلك في "كانَ" لقوّة دلالتها على المضيّ، وأنّها أصلُ الأفعال وعبارتُها، فجاز لذلك أن تقلب في الدلالة "إنْ". ولذلك لا يقع شيءٌ من الأفعال غيرُ "كان" بعد "إن"، إلَّا ومعناه المضارعُ. وقال ابن السرّاج: هو على تأويلِ: "إن أكُنْ كنتُ قُلْتُه"، وكذلك ما كان مثله.

<https://shamela.ws/book/13301/2044>

[[معاني النحو]{.ar} 4/79](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1184#p1) for ;idha, prev pages for [إِنْ]{.ar}


## [كَانَ]{.ar} before the conditional sentence

Fischer 236

(@) [كان إذا اشتكيت رحمني]{.ar}

(@) [كنت إذل قوم غزوني غزوتهم]{.ar}

(@) [كانت العجوز إذا كلمها تسكت عنه]{.ar}

Wright 2/16C


## [فَ]{.ar} as a condition-response-introducer {#sec-j_sh_msc}

So far we have seen that the condition-response in a conditional sentence can take the following main forms:

* A [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} verbal sentence for [إِنْ]{.ar} conditional sentences.                  Negated using [لَا يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}.
* A non-past tense [قَتَلَ]{.ar} verbal sentence for both [إِنْ]{.ar} and [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences.  Negated using [لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}.

(We have also seen the [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar} form but we will treat it separately in section\ (ref) below.)

When the condition-response has either of the above two ([يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} or non-past tense [قَتَلَ]{.ar}) forms,
then it is not introduced by the condition-response-introducer [فَ]{.ar}.
So for example, 
we say:

(@) [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي آتِكَ]{.ar}  
    [If you come to me, I will come to you.]{.trans}

But we cannot say:

(@) ✗ [إِنْ تَأْتِنِي فَآتِكَ]{.ar}

However, when the condition-response falls outside the above two main forms,
then it will typically be introduced by [فَ]{.ar}.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ يَأْتِكَ فَأَكْرِمْهُ]{.ar}  
    [If he comes to you, then honor him.]{.trans}

The condition-response in example () above is a verb of command verbal sentence.
Since it is not a [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} or (non-past tense) [قَتَلَ]{.ar} form,
we use a [فَ]{.ar} to introduce it.

The main reason that a [فَ]{.ar} is needed in such cases,
is to indicate that the condition-response is linked to the condition.
Without it, the condition-response would appear to be an independent sentence
and its meaning would not be tied to the condition being satisfied.
So we cannot (usually) say, without [فَ]{.ar}:

(@) ✗ [إِنْ يَأْتِكَ أَكْرِمْهُ]{.ar}  

[فَ]{.ar} is not needed when the condition-response is a [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} or (non-past tense) [قَتَلَ]{.ar} verbal sentence
because in those cases the link between the condition-response and the condition is readily apparent.
In the case of a [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} condition-response,
the link is indicated by the ø-state of the condition-response-verb,
as in example () above.
And in the case
of a [قَتَلَ]{.ar} condition-response,
it is indicated by the non-past tense meaning of the [قَتَلَ]{.ar} condition-response-verb,
as in the example below:

(@) [إِذَا أَتَيْتَنِي **أَتَيْتُكَ**]{.ar}
    [If you come to me, I will come to you.]{.trans}

<!--
* [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar} (and [لَا يَقْتُلُ]{.ar}) for both [إِنْ]{.ar} and [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences
1. For [إِنْ]{.ar} conditional sentences:
   * [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
   * [قَتَلَ]{.ar} that loses its past tense meaning
   * Occasionally [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar}
2. For [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences:
   * [قَتَلَ]{.ar} that loses its past tense meaning
   * [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar}

---

In this section we will study condition-responses that are introduced by [فَ]{.ar}.
-->
<!--
We will first treat conditional sentences that are introduced by [إِنْ]{.ar}.

### [فَ]{.ar} in the condition-response for [إِنْ]{.ar} conditional sentences
-->

<!--
Here is an example of an [إِنْ]{.ar} conditional sentence whose condition-response begins with [فَ]{.ar}:

(@) [إِنْ أَتَاكَ فَأَكْرِمْهُ]{.ar}  
    [If he comes to you then honor him.]{.trans}  
-->

<!--
[فَ]{.ar}, in the beginning of the condition-response,
explicitly makes it clear that the sentence in the condition-response is
a consequence of the condition.
-->

<!--
So far the conditional sentences that we have studied have not been introduced by [فَ]{.ar}.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ]{.ar}  
    [If you come to me, I will honor you.]{.trans}

This is because the condition-response in such conditional sentences is
a direct consequence
of the condition.
What we mean by
*direct consequence*
is that:

1. The condition-response occurs *because* of the condition being satisfied.
   For example, in example () above, my honoring you is *because of* your coming to me.
   By the way, in some conditional sentences this causality may not be strictly met. For example:

   (@) [فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ ٱلۡكَلۡبِ إِن تَحۡمِلۡ عَلَیۡهِ یَلۡهَثۡ أَوۡ تَتۡرُكۡهُ یَلۡهَثۚ]{.quran}
       [[[سورة الأعراف]{.ar} 7:176](https://quran.com/7/176) cited by [[معاني النحو]{.ar} 4/53](https://shamela.ws/book/17801/1158)]{.citex}  
       [So his example is like that of the dog: if you chase him, he pants, or if you leave him, he [still] pants.]{.trans}  

2. The conditional sentence matches one of the patterns of conditional sentences. These are:
   
   a. For [إِنْ]{.ar} conditional sentences:

        i. [إِنْ يَقْتُلْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
       ii. [إِنْ قَتَلَ قَتَلَ]{.ar}
      iii. [إِنْ قَتَلَ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
       iv. [إِنْ يَقْتُلْ قَتَلَ]{.ar}

   b. For [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences: [إِذَا قَتَلَ قَتَلَ]{.ar}
        
   
   Substitutable in the above patterns are the negative constructions
   [لَا يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
   (in place of [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar})
   and
   [لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
   (in place of [قَتَلَ]{.ar}).
   And also [يَقْتُلُ]{.ar} in place of [قَتَلَ]{.ar} for [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences.


Sometimes,
the condition-response is not a direct consequence (as defined above) of the condition.
Instead,
the condition-response has the force of an independent sentence,
albeit one that is linked, in some respect, to the condition taking place.
-->
<!--
In order to show that the condition-response,
despite its force,
is linked to condition
-->
<!--
Because this link would otherwise not be apparent,
a [فَ]{.ar} is typically required to
explicitly indicate that the condition-response is
related to the occurrence of the condition.
-->

### Types of condition-responses requiring [فَ]{.ar}

Here are the main situations in which
[فَ]{.ar} is used to introduce the condition-response:

#### The condition-response-internal-sentence is a request {.unnumbered}

What we mean by *request* is a command, supplication, entreaty, or prohibition.
When the condition-response-internal-sentence is a request
then it is typically required to be introduced by [فَ]{.ar}.
For example:


##### Direct commands: {.unnumbered}

(@) [وَلاَ تُكَلِّفُوهُمْ مَا يَغْلِبُهُمْ، فَإِنْ كَلَّفْتُمُوهُمْ **فَأَعِينُوهُمْ**]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :30](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:30)]{.citex}  
    [Do not task them with what is too much for them but if you task them, then help them.]{.trans}  

<!--
(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ تَعْلَمُ أَنَّ هَذَا الأَمْرَ خَيْرٌ لِي فِي دِينِي وَمَعَاشِي وَعَاقِبَةِ أَمْرِي ـ أَوْ قَالَ عَاجِلِ أَمْرِي وَآجِلِهِ ـ فَاقْدُرْهُ لِي وَيَسِّرْهُ لِي ثُمَّ بَارِكْ لِي فِيهِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :1166](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1166)]{.citex}  
    -->

(@) [فَإِنْ غُمَّ عَلَيْكُمْ **فَاقْدُرُوا** لَهُ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :1906](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1906)]{.citex}  
    [If it is overcast then estimate for it.]{.trans}

(@) [إِذَا لَمْ تَسْتَحِي **فَافْعَلْ** مَا شِئْتَ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :3483](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3483)]{.citex}  
    [If you do not feel ashamed then do whatever you wish.]{.trans}

(@) [فَإِذَا لَقِيتُمُوهُمْ **فَاصْبِرُوا**]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :2965](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2965)]{.citex}  
    [If you meet them then be patient.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِنْ لَمْ تَجِدِينِي **فَأْتِي** أَبَا بَكْرٍ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :7220](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:7220)]{.citex}  
    [If you~1f~ don't find me then come to [#abU #bakr]{.trn2}.]{.trans}  

##### Supplications: {.unnumbered}

(@) [اللَّهُمَّ إِنْ كُنْتَ تَعْلَمُ فِيَّ خَيْرًا **فَأَرِنِي** رُؤْيَا]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :7028](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:7028)]{.citex}  
    [O Allāh, if you are knowing good in me then show me a dream.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتَ كَاذِبًا **فَصَيَّرَكَ** اللَّهُ إِلَى مَا كُنْتَ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :3464](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3464)]{.citex}  
    [If you are a liar then may Allah make you to what you used to be.]{.trans}

##### Prohibitions: {.unnumbered}

(@) [فَإِنْ وَجَدْتُمْ غَيْرَ آنِيَتِهِمْ **فَلاَ تَأْكُلُوا** فِيهَا]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :5488](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5488)]{.citex}  
    [If you find other than their utensils then don't eat in them]{.trans}  

(@) [وَإِنْ خَالَطَهَا كِلاَبٌ مِنْ غَيْرِهَا **فَلاَ تَأْكُلْ**]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :5483](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5483)]{.citex}  
    [And if other hounds join in it then don't eat [it]]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِذَا سَمِعْتُمْ بِهِ بِأَرْضٍ **فَلاَ تَقْدَمُوا** عَلَيْهِ وَإِذَا وَقَعَ بِأَرْضٍ وَأَنْتُمْ بِهَا **فَلاَ تَخْرُجُوا** فِرَارًا مِنْهُ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :3473](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:3473)]{.citex}  
    [So if you hear of it in a land then don't approach it, and if occurs in a land where you are present, then don't leave [that land] in order to flee from it.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ رَأَيْتُمُونَا ظَهَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ **فَلاَ تَبْرَحُوا** وَإِنْ رَأَيْتُمُوهُمْ ظَهَرُوا عَلَيْنَا **فَلاَ تُعِينُونَا**]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :4043](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4043)]{.citex}  
    [If you see us having overcome them then don't leave [this place], and if you see them having overcome us then don't help us.]{.trans}  

##### Indirect commands: {.unnumbered}

(@) [فَأَيُّكُمْ إِذَا أَرَادَ أَنْ يُوَاصِلَ **فَلْيُوَاصِلْ** حَتَّى السَّحَرِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :1963](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1963)]{.citex}  
    [So whichever of you -- if he wants to continue on, then let him continue on until the last part of the night.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِذَا أَتَى أَحَدَكُمْ خَادِمُهُ بِطَعَامِهِ فَإِنْ لَمْ يُجْلِسْهُ مَعَهُ **فَلْيُنَاوِلْهُ** أُكْلَةً أَوْ أُكْلَتَيْنِ أَوْ لُقْمَةً أَوْ لُقْمَتَيْنِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :5460](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5460)]{.citex}  
    [If the servant of one of you brings him his food, then if he doesn't make him sit with him, then let him hand him a bite or two, or a morsel or two.]{.trans}

<!--
yaqtulu also can be hortative peled 79
-->

##### Indirect prohibitions: {.unnumbered}

(@) [إِذَا شَرِبَ أَحَدُكُمْ **فَلاَ يَتَنَفَّسْ** فِي الإِنَاءِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :5630](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:5630) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 79]{.citex}  
    [When one of you drinks then let him not breather in the vessel.]{.trans}  

(@) [إِذَا كَانَ أَحَدُكُمْ يُصَلِّي، فَلاَ يَبْصُقْ قِبَلَ وَجْهِهِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :406](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:406) cited by Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 79]{.citex}  
    [When one of you is praying then let him not spit in front of him.]{.trans}  

#### The condition-response-internal-sentence begins with a fixed verb {.unnumbered}


(@) [إِنْ يَعِشْ هَذَا الْغُلاَمُ **فَعَسَى** أَنْ لاَ يُدْرِكَهُ الْهَرَمُ حَتَّى تَقُومَ السَّاعَةُ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح مسلم]{.ar} :2953a](https://sunnah.com/muslim:2953a)]{.citex}  
    [If this boy should live then maybe old age will not hit him until the hour is estalished.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِنْ صَدَقَا وَبَيَّنَا بُورِكَ لَهُمَا فِي بَيْعِهِمَا وَإِنْ كَذَبَا وَكَتَمَا **فَعَسَى** أَنْ يَرْبَحَا رِبْحًا وَيُمْحَقَا بَرَكَةَ بَيْعِهِمَا]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :2114](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:2114)]{.citex}  
    [If they speak the truth and mention the defects, then their bargain will be blessed, and if they tell lies and conceal the defects, they might gain some financial gain but they will be deprived of the blessing of their sale.]{.trans}

(@) [فَإِذَا كَانَتْ سَائِمَةُ الرَّجُلِ نَاقِصَةً مِنْ أَرْبَعِينَ شَاةً وَاحِدَةً **فَلَيْسَ** فِيهَا صَدَقَةٌ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :1454](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:1454)]{.citex}  
    [If the man's livestock is less than forty sheep then there is no [zakAh]{.trn2} on it.]{.trans}  

#### The condition-response-internal-sentence is a nounal sentence {.unnumbered}

When the condition-response-internal-sentence

(@) [إِنْ قَتَلْتَ حَمْزَةَ بِعَمِّي **فَأَنْتَ** حُرٌّ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :4072](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4072)]{.citex}  
    [If you kill [#Hamzah]{.trn2} [in revenge] for my uncle, then you are free.]{.trans}  

(@) [فَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ مَا تَشَابَهَ مِنْهُ **فَأُولَئِكَ** الَّذِينَ سَمَّى اللَّهُ، فَاحْذَرُوهُمْ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :4547](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4547)]{.citex}  
    [If you see the people who follow what is open to different interpretations, then they are ones whom Allāh named, so beware of them.]{.trans}  

#### The condition-response-internal-sentence is a question {.unnumbered}

#### The condition-response-internal-sentence signifies an event which already occurred {.unnumbered}

[قَدْ]{.ar} is also typically used in such cases with [فَ]{.ar}.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ تَطْعَنُوا فِي إِمَارَتِهِ **فَقَدْ** طَعَنْتُمْ فِي إِمَارَةِ أَبِيهِ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :4250](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4250)]{.citex}  
    [If you speak ill of his leadership, you have already spoken ill of his father's leadership before.]{.trans}

If [قَدْ]{.ar} is not used in the condition-response, but a past event is still signified,
then too [فَ]{.ar} is used.
For example:

(@) [وَإِن كَانَ قَمِیصُهُۥ قُدَّ مِن دُبُرࣲ **فَكَذَبَتۡ** وَهُوَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِینَ]{.quran}
    [[[سورة يوسف]{.ar} 12:27](https://quran.com/12/27) cited by [معاني النحو]{.ar} 4/105]{.citex}  
    [But if his shirt is torn from the back, then she has lied, and he is of the truthful.]{.trans}  

[فَ]{.ar} is also used with [قَتَلَ]{.ar} in the condition-response when a future perfect event is signified.
For example:

peled p. 80 (28)

<!--
#### The condition uses [كَانَ [قَدْ] قَتَلَ]{.ar} to signify an event which already occurred {.unnumbered}

peled 72--73, wright 2/15

We discuss in section\ (ref) below
that
[كَانَ (قَدْ) قَتَلَ]{.ar}
can be used in the condition to indicate an event which has already occurred.
In such cases,
the condition-response will typically be introduced by [فَ]{.ar}.
For example:
-->

<!--
#### The condition-response-internal-sentence signifies a event which is expected to definitely occur {.unnumbered}

[فَ]{.ar} can be used to introduce a verbal sentence condition-response.
to make it more independent from the condition,
and to signify that the event indicated by the condition-response is expected to definitely occur
(if the condition is met).
-->

#### The condition-response-verb is preceded by a particle other than [لَمْ]{.ar}

When the condition-response-verb is preceded by [لَمْ]{.ar}
then it typically occurs directly in the condition-response without being introduced by [فَ]{.ar}.
But when the condition-response-verb is preceded by another particle
like [سَ]{.ar}, [سَوْفَ]{.ar}, or [لَنْ]{.ar},
then a [فَ]{.ar} is required.
For example:

(@) [إِنْ كُنْتِ بَرِيئَةً **فَسَيُبَرِّئُكِ** اللَّهُ وَإِنْ كُنْتِ أَلْمَمْتِ بِذَنْبٍ فَاسْتَغْفِرِي اللَّهَ وَتُوبِي إِلَيْهِ]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح البخاري]{.ar} :4690](https://sunnah.com/bukhari:4690)]{.citex}  

(@) [إِن تَسۡتَغۡفِرۡ لَهُمۡ سَبۡعِینَ مَرَّةࣰ **فَلَن** یَغۡفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمۡۚ]{.quran}
    [[[سورة التوبة]{.ar} 9:80](https://quran.com/9/80)]{.citex}  
    [If you should ask forgiveness for them seventy times - never will Allāh forgive them.]{.trans}  

Once [فَ]{.ar} introduces the condition-response,
[مَا]{.ar} then can be used to negate a verb in the condition-response.
For example:


<!--
Emphasis is on the occurrence (or non-occurrence) of condition-response.
[فَ]{.ar} is then needed to indicate that even though emphasis is on the occurrence of condition-response,
it is still linked to condition.

#### Summary {.unnumbered}

Generally, [فَ]{.ar} is added to the condition-response,
when it may not be apparent that condition-response is linked to condition.
So [فَ]{.ar} makes it obvious.
-->

<!--
### [فَ]{.ar} in the condition-response for [إِذَا]{.ar} conditional sentences
-->

### [إِذَا]{.ar} fujaaiyyah instead of [فَ]{.ar}

### Government of the condition-response that begins with [فَ]{.ar}

### Omitting the [فَ]{.ar} when it is required

Peled, _Conditional structures in Classical Arabic_ 77--78

### Adding a [فَ]{.ar} when it is not required



---



The fact that the 
condition-response is a direct consequence of the condition
is shown by:

1. The pattern of the conditional sentence.
1. The matching time-reference of meanings of the condition and the condition-response.

When the condition-response is independent of the condition,
then a [فَ]{.ar} is typically required to
explicitly indicate that the condition-response is
a consequence of the condition.

---


This is because the patterns of the conditional sentence, that we have studied so far,
implcitly indicate that the condition-response is a consequence of the condition.
These patterns, as we know, are:

1. [إِنْ يَقْتُلْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
1. [إِنْ قَتَلَ قَتَلَ]{.ar}
1. [إِنْ قَتَلَ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
1. [إِنْ يَقْتُلْ قَتَلَ]{.ar}

Also included in the above patterns are the negative constructions
[لَا يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
(in place of [يَقْتُلْ]{.ar})
and
[لَمْ يَقْتُلْ]{.ar}
(in place of [قَتَلَ]{.ar}).

When an [إِنْ]{.ar} conditional sentence falls into one of the above patterns
and 

When the condition-response falls outside the above patterns,
then a [فَ]{.ar} is typically required to
explicitly indicate that the condition-response is
a consequence of the condition.

---

For example:

In the types of conditional sentences that we have studied so far,
the condition-response is a verbal sentence that begins

When the condition-response is a verbal sentence
and the verb in the condition-response is the direct consequence of the condition,
then no intervening [فَ]{.ar} is needed.
Otherwise, a [فَ]{.ar} is usually needed to make it clear that the condition-response is a consequence of the condition.

## The condition-introducer nouns that govern like [إِنْ]{.ar}

<https://shamela.ws/book/98123/69#p1>:

::: {.ar}
ومن تشبيه "متى" بـ "إذا" وإهمالها قول عائشة رضي الله عنها (إن أبا بكر رجلٌ أسيف، وإنه (١١٨) متى يقومُ مقامك لا يُسمعُ الناس) (١١٩)
:::


## The time-reference of a conditional sentence

For conditional sentences which have a condition-verb or condition-response-verb,
the time-reference of either of these two verbs
is determined by context, regardless of whether the verb is in the
[يَقْتُلْ]{.ar} or [قَتَلَ]{.ar} form.

Most contexts will steer the time-reference to the future.
For example:

in ataytani akramtuka

in ta'tini ukrimka



### The time-reference and significance of dynamic verbs in a conditional sentence

Dynamic verbs are those that state an event or describe a process,
like [أَتَى]{.ar} [to come]{.trans},
[أَكْرَمَ]{.ar} [to honor]{.trans},
etc.


<!--
So far we have deferred a detailed discussion
of the time-reference indicated by the condition and the condition-response.
In this section, we will try to do justice to the topic, if Allāh wills.
-->

Earlier we had summarily stated that
the time-reference signified
in conditional sentences is generally the future time-reference,
regardless of what form of verb
(for example: ø-state or stateless)
is used.

However, there can be instances of
of a
condition-verb
or a
condition-response-verb
having a past time-reference.
For example:

(@) [قَالُوۤا۟ إِن **یَسۡرِقۡ** فَقَدۡ سَرَقَ أَخࣱ لَّهُۥ مِن قَبۡلُۚ]{.quran}^[Ibn\ [#mAlik]{.trn2} and [#abU #HayyAn]{.trn2} interpret the condition in this [Ayah]{.trn2} as having a past time-reference.  See [[شواهد التوضيح لابن مالك]{.ar} 231](https://shamela.ws/book/98123/228) and [[البحر المحيط لأبي حيان]{.ar}](https://tafsir.app/albahr-almuheet/12/77) for [سورة يوسف]{.ar} 12:77 respectively.]
    [[[سورة يوسف]{.ar} 12:77](https://quran.com/12/77)]{.citex}  
    [They said, "If he steals - a brother of his has stolen before."]{.trans}

(@) [وَإِذَا **رَأَوۡا۟** تِجَـٰرَةً أَوۡ لَهۡوًا **ٱنفَضُّوۤا۟** إِلَیۡهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَاۤىِٕمࣰاۚ]{.quran}
    [[[سورة الجمعة]{.ar} 62:11](https://quran.com/62/11)]{.citex}  
    [But [on one occasion] when they **saw** a transaction or a diversion, [O Muḥammad], they **rushed** to it and left you standing.]{.trans}  

(@) [أَنَّ النَّبِيَّ صلى الله عليه وسلم كَانَ إِذَا أُتِيَ بِطَعَامٍ سَأَلَ عَنْهُ **فَإِنْ قِيلَ** هَدِيَّةٌ **أَكَلَ** مِنْهَا **وَإِنْ قِيلَ** صَدَقَةٌ **لَمْ يَأْكُلْ** مِنْهَا]{.ar}
    [[[صحيح مسلم]{.ar} :1077](https://sunnah.com/muslim:1077)]{.citex}  
    [that the Prophet [ﷺ]{.aralt} would, when he was brought some food, ask about it. If it **was said** [that it was] a gift, he **ate** from it, and if it **was said** [that it was] charity, he **did not eat** from it.]{.trans}  

So how do we relate the forms of the
condition-verb
and the condition-response-verb
to the time-reference signified by the conditional sentence?
We will attempt to explain this below:

As you know,
the following positive verb forms are used in conditional sentences:

1. The ø-state stateful verb

   (@) [إِنْ **تَأْتِنِي أُكْرِمْكَ**]{.ar}  

2. The stateless verb. For example:

   (@) [إِنْ **أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ**]{.ar}  

   (@) [إِذَا  **أَتَيْتَنِي أَكْرَمْتُكَ**]{.ar}  

3. The u-state stateful verb.

   (@) [إِنْ أَتَيْتَنِي **أُكْرِمُكَ**]{.ar}  

   (@) [إِذَا **تَأْتِينِي أُكْرِمُكَ**]{.ar}  

Not all combinations of the verb forms
are shown in examples () through () above.
Other combinations,
and their relative frequencies of usage,
have been discussed
in sections\ (refs) above.

<!--
The question is,
when is one verb form chosen over another,
and what difference in meaning would it signify?

First, we would like to state
that in many cases the verb forms,
when permissible,
are often interchangeable.
And the nuances in meaning that we will attempt to explain below
are more
connotations and trends
rather than hard and fast rules.
Needless to say, they are also based on our subjective reasoning,
and therefore liable to critique.
-->

We will begin by first listing the essential significances of each of the above verb forms
independent of conditional sentences.
We will then explain how those essential significances can carry over to within conditional sentences.

The essential signification of the
stateless verb form
is that an action
has reached a completed state.
So for example, if we say
[أَتَيْتَنِي]{.ar},
then the stateless verb form signifies that
your action of coming to me has been completed.
The stateless verb has the most certainty of the different verb forms,
because any action that has completed has certainly occurred.

The essential signification of the
u-state stateful verb form
is that
an action
is either on-going,
or that it will occur in the future.
So for example, if we say
[تَأْتِينِي]{.ar},
then the u-state stateful verb form signifies that
your action of coming to me is either on-going,
or that it will occur in the future.

The essential signification of the positive ø-state stateful verb
is that the speaker
considers an action
to be potentially possible.
So for example, if we say
[تَأْتِنِي]{.ar},
then the ø-state stateful verb form indicates
that your coming to me is a potential possibility.^[[التأويل الزمني للجملة الشرطية في اللغة العربية لعارف الدين]{.ar} 49]
<!--
^[[التأويل الزمني للجملة الشرطية في اللغة العربية لعارف الدين ٤٩ (يستشهد أحمد المتوكل، قضايا اللغة العربية في اللسانيات الوظيفية البنية التحتية أو التمثيل الداللي التداولي. الرباط: دار الأمان، ١٩٩٥.)]{.ar}]
-->

The ø-state stateful verb has less certainty compared to the other verb forms
due to its signifying a potential action.

When these verb forms are used in the condition
or the condition-response,
then their essential significations,
as listed above,
can be retained.
But the condition-introducer allows the time-reference of the condition to be open to all times.

<!--
But the condition-introducer can allow the scenario of the condition,
i.e. the situation in which the condition occurs,
to be transported to a different time-reference.
By default, it will transport the scenario of condition to the future,
but it could also be transported to the past.
-->
<!--
The time-reference of the
entire condition, however,
could be in the past, present, or future.
-->

So,
<!--
if we use a stateless verb
as a condition-verb or a condition-response-verb
then it will indicate a completed action.
The time-reference is determined by when the scenario of condition will occur.
-->
if the scenario of the condition occurred in the past,
then the conditional sentence will have a past time-reference.
For example^[The following "Nile delta" examples are adapted from Reilly, J.\ S.\ "The acquisition of temporals and conditionals", In: Traugott, et al, (eds.) _On Conditionals_, Cambridge, 1986, p.\ 313, cited by Alfraidi, _Conditional sentences in Modern Written Arabic_ p.\ 94.]:

(@) [إِنْ مَطَرَتْ مِصْرُ عَامَ الْأَوَّلِ سَالَتْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it rained in Egypt last year, the Nile delta [must have] flooded.]{.trans}

(@) [إِنْ تَمْطَرْ مِصْرُ عَامَ الْأَوَّلِ تَسِلْ بَطَائِحُ النِّيلِ]{.ar}  
    [If it [conceivably] rained in Egypt last year, the  Nile delta [should have] flooded.]{.trans}
